facts_disclosed: Quit hot yoga entirely, got into rock climbing instead

turns.content: I completely dropped yoga btw. got into climbing and it's so much better for my brain — the problem solving aspect is like a puzzle. I went for the first time like three weeks ago and I'm kind of obsessed

Oh that's such a quick conversion — three weeks in and obsessed is the classic climbing trajectory. The puzzle thing is exactly why it hooks people who have technical or analytical brains: you're not just exercising, you're *solving*, and when you send a route it's a completely different feeling than just finishing a workout.

Are you doing bouldering or top rope/lead, and are you going to a gym or outdoors? And what level are you starting at — total beginner or did you have some prior experience?

total beginner, just bouldering at a gym for now. I started on V0s and V1s and I'm just starting to do some V2s. I don't really know what I'm doing lol but every time I figure out a problem I feel like I solved a math problem

V2 in three weeks is solid, especially as a complete beginner — that's faster than average. The math-problem feeling is real and it only gets more intense as you go up the grades, because the harder problems require actual beta reading (figuring out the sequence of moves before you try) rather than just muscling through.

Few beginner things that will help you progress faster and also not hurt yourself:

**Most important: learn to fall/come off properly.** It sounds basic but a lot of beginners hold on too long when pumped because letting go feels scary, and then they fall badly when their hands give out. Practice stepping down or letting go intentionally before you're exhausted.

**Footwork over arm strength:** Every beginner thinks climbing is about arm strength. It's mostly feet. If your toes are placed precisely on holds you save enormous energy. Slabs (less than vertical walls) are great for forcing good footwork.

**Climb with your hips close to the wall.** Beginner instinct is to lean away from the wall with a death grip — it's the opposite of what works.

Are you going alone or with someone? And has anyone given you any intro instruction yet?

I went with a coworker the first time and she showed me the basics, but mostly I've been going alone. the footwork thing is already something I can tell I'm bad at, like I keep defaulting to just pulling with my arms and then I'm wrecked after like 3 problems

The pump-out-after-3-problems thing is almost entirely a forearm endurance issue combined with over-gripping. When you're new, your brain doesn't trust your feet on holds so your hands grip way harder than necessary to compensate, and your forearms fatigue super fast. This is normal and it gets better as your feet learn to trust holds, but you can also actively practice it.

A drill that works really well: climb problems that are below your limit (V0-V1) but try to make each move as precise and foot-focused as possible. The goal isn't to finish the problem quickly, it's to place your feet deliberately on every move. Almost exaggeratedly precise. It feels slow but it builds the habit and your forearms will thank you.

Also: rest between problems. 3-5 minutes between attempts on hard stuff, not just 30 seconds. Forearms recover on a different timeline than legs — if you're going immediately to the next problem when your forearms are still pumped, you're just training bad habits on tired muscles.

The fact that you're already at a gym and going regularly is the main thing. Technique stuff comes with reps, and you're clearly putting in the reps.

the over-gripping thing makes so much sense, I definitely do that. also I need to figure out gear — right now I'm just renting shoes from the gym which is fine but I know renting long-term is annoying and also kind of gross lol

Rental shoes are... fine... for getting started, but yes, they're generally beat up, stretched out, and you can never quite trust the fit. Getting your own shoes makes a real difference, both in feel and in not wondering about the previous forty feet that were in them.

For beginner bouldering shoes, the key advice: **don't size down aggressively.** There's a culture in climbing of getting shoes painfully tight for performance reasons, and this is for experienced climbers on hard routes. As a beginner learning footwork, shoes that fit comfortably (snug but not painful, toes slightly curled but not mashed) will teach you better technique than shoes you're desperate to take off.

Good beginner shoes that people consistently recommend:
- **La Sportiva Tarantulace** (~$80): Lace-up, very comfortable, good for learning. Forgiving fit.
- **Scarpa Origin** (~$85): Similar category, also very well reviewed for beginners.
- **Black Diamond Momentum** (~$75): Good value, comfortable, velcro (easier on/off).

All of these are at REI, or you can order online. If you can, try them on in person because fit is very individual.

Do you need anything else beyond shoes? Chalk and a chalk bag are nice to have but not urgent at the beginner stage.
I've been borrowing chalk from my coworker lmao. is chalk actually necessary or is it more of a thing people just do

It's genuinely functional, not just ritual — chalk absorbs sweat and moisture from your hands, which improves friction on holds. Whether you 'need' it depends partly on how much your hands sweat and partly on the holds. Smooth or polished holds, especially in a gym with lots of traffic, are noticeably better with chalk. Rougher holds on wood or textured surfaces you can get away without it more.

For gym bouldering specifically, chalk is pretty standard and most gyms have it. Buying your own is cheap: a chalk bag is like $10-15 (REI has fine basic ones), and a block of chalk is maybe $3-5. You break off a chunk and put it in the bag.

One gear note: some gyms require loose chalk to be in a chalk ball (a mesh ball you squeeze rather than dipping) to keep chalk dust down. Check if your gym has a policy on this — the bag and ball version is easy to find together.

So your starter gear list is basically: your own shoes, chalk bag, chalk or chalk ball. Total investment maybe $90-110. Everything else (harness, belay device, etc.) is only relevant if you move to top rope or lead, which is not a beginner concern.

Which gym in Austin are you going to? There are a few good options and some have better beginner communities than others.
I'm going to Austin Bouldering Project. it's pretty close to where I live which is the main reason lol. is that one any good?

Austin Bouldering Project is well-regarded — it's one of the more popular facilities there, has good route setting, and tends to have an active community. The proximity factor is honestly the most important variable for whether someone sticks with climbing, so you've got that right.

One thing to look for if they have it: a 'beginner circuit' or introductory route set. A lot of gyms mark a set of problems specifically designed for learning movement and technique, separate from the main grading. Worth asking at the front desk if you haven't already — can be a more structured way to build the fundamentals than just hunting for V0s.

Also: ABP sometimes does beginner clinics or intro sessions. Those can be worth doing once even if you've been going a few weeks, because you might pick up some technique cues from an instructor that would take months to figure out on your own.

Does it feel like you're going to stick with this, or are you still in the 'let's see' phase?

no I think I'm sticking with it lol. like I've dropped a lot of fitness things over the years but this feels different because it doesn't feel like 'going to exercise,' it feels like 'going to solve puzzles.' the motivation is completely different

That's the exact distinction that predicts long-term adherence — when the intrinsic motivation is the activity itself rather than 'I should exercise.' The people who climb for years aren't disciplined about fitness, they're just addicted to the puzzle.

Yoga, for all its benefits, is a very different kind of thing — it asks you to be present and still in a way that works well for some people and feels like work for others. For someone with a pattern-recognition and problem-solving brain, climbing is honestly a more natural fit.

Buy the shoes soon — they'll make those V2s easier and your feet will be happier. And welcome to the part of your personality that explains why a lot of ML engineers end up climbing.
lmao at the ml engineers end up climbing comment, I feel seen. ok gonna go this weekend and look at shoes. thanks for all the tips
Enjoy! The shoe shopping is the first act of fully committing to the obsession. Have fun at ABP and good luck on those V2s.
